beats.narration_text: Salaam, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. Cancer disparities — why ZIP code predicts survival more than tumor biology.

Black patients with breast cancer have a 40 percent higher mortality rate than white patients despite similar incidence. The biology of the tumors is genuinely different — higher triple-negative rate — but biology explains only part of the gap. Access, screening timing, treatment quality, and clinical trial representation each contribute. Which of these factors, when fixed, actually closes the gap?

beats.narration_text: Research racial disparities in breast cancer outcomes: the Black-white mortality gap, what fraction is attributable to biology versus structural factors, which specific interventions have demonstrated gap reduction, and NCI Cancer Disparities Research Program priorities.

props.command: claude "Research racial disparities in breast cancer outcomes: the Black-white mortality gap (absolute and relative), what fraction is attributable to biology (triple-negative breast cancer prevalence), what fraction is attributable to structural factors (screening access, treatment delays, financial toxicity, clinical trial enrollment), which specific interventions have demonstrated gap reduction in published trials, and what the NCI Cancer Disparities Research Program priorities are."

beats.narration_text: Python script building causal chain analysis with attributable mortality fraction and intervention evidence per factor.

props.code:
FACTORS = [
    {"factor": "TNBC biology (higher prevalence)", "mortality_contribution_pct": 25,
     "structural": False, "intervention": "Targeted chemo (carboplatin)", "evidence": "Phase 3 RCT"},
    {"factor": "Stage at diagnosis (later)",       "mortality_contribution_pct": 30,
     "structural": True,  "intervention": "Community mammography access", "evidence": "Observational"},
    {"factor": "Treatment delays (>60 days)",      "mortality_contribution_pct": 15,
     "structural": True,  "intervention": "Patient navigation programs", "evidence": "Controlled"},
    {"factor": "Treatment quality gaps",           "mortality_contribution_pct": 20,
     "structural": True,  "intervention": "Equal-access care centers", "evidence": "Comparative"},
    {"factor": "Trial underenrollment",            "mortality_contribution_pct": 10,
     "structural": True,  "intervention": "Trial diversity requirements", "evidence": "Policy"},
]
for f in FACTORS:
    kind = "STRUCTURAL" if f["structural"] else "biological"
    print(f"[{kind:<11}] {f['factor']:<35}  ~{f['mortality_contribution_pct']}% of gap")
    print(f"             Intervention: {f['intervention']} ({f['evidence']})")

beats.narration_text: Causal chain output — each factor with attributable mortality fraction and intervention evidence tier.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Causal chain output — each factor with attributable mortality fraction and intervention evidence tier.

beats.narration_text: For each structural factor, what is the single highest-evidence intervention that reduces it — and in which health systems has it been implemented at scale with documented outcome improvement?

props.command: claude "For each structural factor, what is the single highest-evidence intervention that reduces it — and in which health systems has it been implemented at scale with documented outcome improvement?"

beats.narration_text: Revised output with scaled intervention evidence — what works and where it has been proven at scale.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Revised output with scaled intervention evidence — what works and where it has been proven at scale.

beats.narration_text: Biology explains some of the gap but not most of it. Triple-negative breast cancer is more common in Black women and is harder to treat — that is real. But when researchers control for stage at diagnosis and treatment quality, the biological disadvantage shrinks significantly. The gap that remains after equalization is the structural gap — and it is addressable. Screening access, financial toxicity removal, and clinical trial enrollment equity are all interventions that exist and work where they have been tried.

beats.narration_text: Read Newman et al. 2021 in NEJM and find the specific analysis comparing outcomes in Black and white patients at equal-access cancer centers versus community hospitals. The differential is the structural contribution made legible — when access is equalized, the gap narrows measurably.
